results.rendered_html: vi-darbha m. ‘destitute of Darbha grass’, N. of a country south of the Vindhya hills (now called Berar; it was the country of Damayantī, wife of Nala; the soil was probably grassless and arid, but the absence of Darbha is said to be due to the fact that the son of a saint died of the prick of a sharp blade of that grass),  MBh. ;  Kāv.  &c.

results.rendered_html:
vidarbha 1〉 m. pl. N. pr. eines Volkes und des von ihm bewohnten Landes, südlich vom Vindhya mit der Hauptstadt Kuṇḍina,  AV. PARIŚ. _in_ Verz. d. B. H. 93 (56) .  MBH. 3,2076 . 2093 . 2772 . 2852  (an den beiden letzten Stellen nach der Lesart der  ed. Bomb. , ˚darbhā  ed. Calc. ). 6,351  ( VP. 187 ). 12,9813 .  R. 4,41,16 .  RAGH. 5,60 .  VARĀH. BṚH. S. 14,8 .  MĀRK. P. 58,17 . sg. das Land: vidarbho nāma janapadaḥ  DAŚAK. 180,9 . vidarbhādhipa  MBH. 3,2409 . vidarbhādhiparājadhānī  RAGH. 5,40 . vidarbhādhipati  BHĀG. P. 10,53,16 . ˚rāja  R. GORR. 1,40,3 .  NAIṢ. 1,50 . ˚pati  MĀLAV. 77 . ˚bhū  NAIṢ. 2,16 .  Verz. d. Oxf. H. 213,b, No. 507 . ˚nagarī  MBH. 3,2094 . 2780 .  HARIV. 5842 .
— 2〉 m. sg. ein Fürst der Vidarbha (vgl. vaidarbha): kanyā vidarbhasya  MBH. 3,2103 . ˚tanayā 2412 . ˚subhrū  NAIṢ. 1,82 . ˚rājadhānī  Verz. d. Oxf. H. 258,a,28 .
— 3〉 m. sg. N. pr. eines Mannes  HARIV. 9569 .  BHĀG. P. 4,28 . ein Sohn Jyāmaghaʼs  HARIV. 1988. fg.  6588 .  VP. 422 .  BHĀG. P. 9,23,38 . 24,1 . ein Sohn Ṛṣabhaʼs 5,4,10 .
— 4〉 m. = vaidarbha Krankheit des Zahnfleisches  ŚĀRṄG. SAṂH. 1,7,76 .
— 5〉 f. ā N. pr.
a〉 einer Stadt, = kuṇḍina  H. 979 .  MBH. 3,2772. fgg.  2826. fgg.  2852  (an allen Stellen m. pl. die  ed. Bomb. ).  HARIV. 6588 .
— b〉 eines Flusses  HARIV. 9511 .
— c〉 einer Tochter Ugraʼs und Gattin des Manu Cākṣuṣa  MĀRK. P. 76,47 .
— Vgl. dadhi˚, daśī˚ und vaidarbha, vaidarbhi.

results.rendered_html:
vidarbha The modern Berār, a great kingdom in ancient times lying to the north of Kuntala and extending from the banks of the Kṛṣṇā to about the banks of
(pb) the Narmadā. On account of its great size, the country was also called ‘Mahārāṣṭra’; cf.  B. R. 10. 74.  Kuṇḍinapura, also called Vidarbhā, was its ancient capital, which probably stands for the modern Beder. The river Varadā (Warda) divided Vidarbha into two parts, Amarāvatī being the capital of the northern, and Pratiṣṭhāna of the southern, part.
